title: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections
authors: Joseph Pechacek, Carol A. Kauffman, Michail S. Lionakis

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Paracoccidioidomycosis
items.content: Paracoccidioidomycosis is caused by thermally dimorphic fungi found in humid regions of Central and South America, with the highest incidence in Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, and Ecuador. The disease predominantly affects middle-aged or elderly men from rural areas due to environmental exposure.
keyPoints: Paracoccidioides brasiliensis complex is endemic to humid areas of Central and South America.
Highest incidence observed in Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, and Ecuador.
Male-to-female ratio ranges from 14:1 to 70:1 due to chronic disease predominance.
Most cases occur in middle-aged or elderly men from rural areas.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Paracoccidioidomycosis
items.content: Paracoccidioidomycosis presents in two forms: acute, seen mostly in individuals under 30 years old with disseminated infection, and chronic, accounting for ~90% of cases affecting older men. Chronic form leads to progressive pulmonary disease and mucocutaneous lesions.
keyPoints: Acute form is uncommon, affects persons <30 years old, manifests as disseminated infection.
Chronic form accounts for ~90% of cases, primarily affects older men.
Manifestations include progressive pulmonary disease with fibrosis and mucocutaneous lesions.
Metastatic foci can involve CNS, skin, or adrenal glands.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Paracoccidioidomycosis
items.content: Paracoccidioidomycosis is diagnosed by culturing the mold form at room temperature or identifying yeast in tissue biopsies. Itraconazole is preferred for treatment, with amphotericin B used initially for seriously ill patients.
keyPoints: Diagnosis via culture of mold form or identification of yeast in biopsy specimens.
Itraconazole is the treatment of choice; voriconazole and posaconazole are also effective.
Amphotericin B recommended for initial treatment of severely ill patients.
Pulmonary fibrosis may progress despite excellent response to therapy.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Talaromycosis
items.content: Talaromycosis, caused by the fungus Talaromyces marneffei, is endemic in southeastern Asia. It primarily affects immunocompromised individuals, especially those with advanced AIDS. Other risk factors include corticosteroid use, solid organ transplantation, idiopathic CD4 lymphocytopenia, and defects in IL-12/IFN-$ \gamma $ signaling.
keyPoints: Endemic to southeastern Asia (Vietnam, Thailand).
Most cases occur in immunocompromised individuals, particularly those with advanced AIDS.
Other risk factors: corticosteroid use, solid organ transplantation, idiopathic CD4 lymphocytopenia, IL-12/IFN-$ \gamma $ signaling defects.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · TALAROMYCOSIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Talaromycosis
items.content: Talaromycosis is diagnosed by culturing T. marneffei from blood or biopsy samples, with characteristic red pigment production in culture. Histopathology shows yeast-like organisms with bipolar fission. Disseminated talaromycosis has a high mortality rate (~10%) if untreated. Itaconazole is preferred for mild/moderate cases; severe cases require AmB followed by triazoles. Suppressive therapy with itaconazole is recommended for AIDS patients until CD4+ T cell count >100 cells/μL for 6 months.
keyPoints: Diagnosis: culture of T. marneffei, red pigment in agar, histopathology showing bipolar fission.
Mortality rate ~10% if untreated.
Treatment: itaconazole for mild/moderate; AmB then triazoles for severe.
Suppressive therapy with itaconazole recommended for AIDS patients until CD4+ >100 cells/μL for 6 months.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · TALAROMYCOSIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Identification of Emergomyces Infections
items.content: Emergomyces infections are diagnosed by culturing the organism from blood or tissue samples, with definitive identification achieved on mold phase in culture. Histopathology may show intracellular and extracellular yeasts that resemble Histoplasma capsulatum, necessitating careful differentiation.
keyPoints: Culturing Emergomyces species can be done within 7 days using aerobic blood culture bottles; fungal isolator culture bottles might improve sensitivity.
Histopathology reveals intracellular and extracellular round or ovoid yeasts with narrow-based budding, which may be misidentified as Histoplasma capsulatum.
The commercially available Histoplasma antigen test has high cross-reactivity with Emergomyces species.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · EMERGOMYCOSIS (DISSEMINATED EMMONSIOSIS)

items.title: Treatment Strategy for Disseminated Emergomyces Infections
items.content: Due to the lack of clinical trials, treatment recommendations for emergomycosis are based on expert opinion and align with those for disseminated histoplasmosis. Initial therapy involves AmB followed by a mold-active azole such as itraconazole.
keyPoints: Immunosuppressed patients should start treatment with AmB until clinical improvement, then switch to a mold-active azole.
Itraconazole is commonly used, but voriconazole and posaconazole have shown favorable in vitro susceptibility results.
For mild disease, itraconazole monotherapy has been successfully reported.
Prolonged secondary prophylaxis may be necessary for patients without a reversible cause of immunodeficiency.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · EMERGOMYCOSIS (DISSEMINATED EMMONSIOSIS)

items.title: Adiaspiromycosis: Recognizing a Rare Fungal Infection
items.content: Adiaspiromycosis is an uncommon fungal infection caused by Emmonia crescens, primarily affecting the lungs. It occurs after inhalation of conidia from soil dust, leading to noncaseating granulomas and adiaspores in tissues. Diagnosis relies on histopathologic identification due to the inability to culture E. crescens from human tissue.
keyPoints: Adiaspiromycosis is caused by Emmonia crescens found in soil.
Major risk factor: occupational dust exposure.
Most common organ affected: lungs, with rare ocular and appendiceal cases.
Diagnosis depends on histopathologic identification of adiaspores surrounded by granulomas.
Adiaspores measure up to 500 µm in diameter.
Clinical sequelae include respiratory failure due to the inflammatory response.
Treatment may involve corticosteroids and antifungal therapy for severe cases.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · ADIASPIROMYCOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.title: Mold Infections: Key Pathogens and Clinical Implications
items.content: Molds cause infections through inhalation or skin inoculation, forming hyphae in tissues. Aspergillosis and mucormycosis are common invasive mold infections. Dematiaceous molds lead to phaeohyphomycoses, often disseminated in immunocompromised patients. Hyaline molds like Fusarium, Scedosporium, and Lomentospora mimic aspergillosis clinically.
keyPoints: Molds cause infection via inhalation or traumatic skin inoculation.
Common invasive mold infections include aspergillosis and mucormycosis.
Dematiaceous molds cause phaeohyphomycoses, often disseminated in immunocompromised patients.
Hyaline molds (Fusarium, Scedosporium, Lomentospora) are prominent pathogens among immunocompromised patients.
Infections with these fungi mimic aspergillosis clinically and histopathologically.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · ADIASPIROMYCOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.title: Understanding Phaeohyphomycosis in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Phaeohyphomycosis, caused by melanin-producing fungi, often leads to localized subcutaneous infections but can disseminate, particularly in immunocompromised individuals. Notably, ~70% of immunocompetent patients with disseminated and brain phaeohyphomycosis carry deleterious variants in CLEC7A.
keyPoints: Phaeohyphomycosis is caused by melanin-producing fungi.
~70% of immunocompetent patients with disseminated and brain infections have deleterious CLEC7A variants.
Immunocompromised individuals are at higher risk for dissemination.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.title: Diagnostic Approaches for Phaeohyphomycosis
items.content: Diagnosis of phaeohyphomycosis involves culturing the organism and histological examination. Melanized hyphal forms appear golden-brown on H&E-stained biopsy specimens, with specialized melanin stains like Fontana-Masson providing additional support.
keyPoints: Culture growth is essential for specific diagnosis.
Histology shows golden-brown melanized hyphae on H&E stain.
Fontana-Masson stain highlights melanized fungal structures.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Phaeohyphomycosis
items.content: Surgical debridement and antifungal therapy are crucial for treating phaeohyphomycosis. Voriconazole is preferred for CNS infections due to its adequate brain concentrations, while posaconazole serves as an alternative.
keyPoints: Surgical debridement is vital in treatment.
Voriconazole is preferred for CNS involvement.
Posaconazole can be used as an alternative azole.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.title: Risk Factors and Pathogenesis of Disseminated Fusariosis
items.content: Disseminated fusariosis predominantly occurs in profoundly immunocompromised patients, particularly those with neutropenia. Neutrophils play a crucial role in defense against Fusarium infections, and their absence or dysfunction significantly increases susceptibility.
keyPoints: Fusarium species are major opportunistic pathogens in markedly immunocompromised patients.
Disseminated infection is most common in patients with hematologic malignancies, neutropenia, post-transplantation, corticosteroid use, or severe burn wounds.
Neutrophils are critical for protection against fusariosis; profound and prolonged neutropenia correlates with susceptibility to disseminated infection.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Fusariosis
items.content: In immunocompromised patients, fusariosis presents as an angioinvasive disease with clinical manifestations similar to aspergillosis. Key features include persistent fever in neutropenic patients and skin lesions that are common and may develop concurrently with fever.
keyPoints: Fusarium causes localized infections in immunocompetent individuals, such as keratitis and onychomycosis.
In immunocompromised patients, fusariosis is angioinvasive, mimicking aspergillosis.
Pulmonary infection presents with multiple nodular lesions visible on CT scan.
Skin lesions are extremely common in disseminated fusariosis and may develop concurrently with fever.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Disseminated Fusariosis
items.content: Diagnosing disseminated fusariosis involves culturing Fusarium species from tissues or blood, with positive blood cultures in up to 50% of cases. Treatment includes antifungal agents like lipid formulations of AmB, voriconazole, or posaconazole, though resistance is common.
keyPoints: Diagnosis requires growth of Fusarium species and histopathologic demonstration of invasion.
Blood cultures are positive in up to 50% of disseminated fusariosis cases.
Fusarium species show resistance to many antifungal agents; lipid AmB, voriconazole, or posaconazole is recommended.
Mortality rates for disseminated fusariosis can be as high as 85%, but improved therapy has reduced this to ~50%.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Emerging Antifungal Agents for Fusariosis Treatment
items.content: Two investigational antifungal agents, fosmanogepix and olorofim, show promise in treating fusariosis. Fosmanogepix inhibits fungal Gwt1 and glycosylphosphatidylinositol-mannoprotein synthesis, demonstrating activity against resistant strains of F. solani. Olorofim targets fungal dihydroorotate dehydrogenase for pyrimidine synthesis, with strain-specific efficacy.
keyPoints: Fosmanogepix inhibits Gwt1 and glycosylphosphatidylinositol-mannoprotein synthesis.
Olorofim inhibits fungal dihydroorotate dehydrogenase.
Both agents show activity against F. solani, with fosmanogepix effective against resistant strains.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 2) | SCEDOSPORIOSIS AND LOMENTOSPORIOSIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Manifestations of Scedosporiosis and Lomentosporiosis
items.content: Scedosporium apiospermum complex and Lomentospora prolificans are emerging pathogens causing infections in immunocompromised hosts. Diagnosis relies on culture growth from infected tissue and histologic evidence of acute-angle septate hyphae. Infections can mimic aspergillosis radiographically, with nodules, cavities, and lobar infiltrates common findings.
keyPoints: Scedosporium apiospermum complex is more frequently reported than Lomentospora prolificans.
Infection occurs through inhalation or direct inoculation.
Diagnosis requires culture growth and histologic demonstration of tissue invasion.
Radiographic findings mimic aspergillosis with nodules, cavities, and lobar infiltrates.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 2) | SCEDOSPORIOSIS AND LOMENTOSPORIOSIS

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.question: Which environmental conditions are most associated with the highest incidence of Paracoccidioides infections?
options: Dry areas with low rainfall and desert landscapes
Humid areas with high rainfall and tropical or subtropical forests
Urban environments with significant air pollution
items.explanation: Paracoccidioides infections are most common in humid areas characterized by high rainfall and the presence of tropical or subtropical forests.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of the yeast form of Paracoccidioides in tissue biopsy specimens?
options: Multiple small buds attached to a central cell via broad connections
A single large bud with no distinct structure
Central yeast with multiple daughter cells attached circumferentially via narrow-necked buds
Irregularly shaped clusters without any specific pattern
items.explanation: The distinctive thick-walled yeast in tissue biopsy specimens appears as a central yeast with multiple daughter cells attached circumferentially via narrow-necked buds, resembling a mariner's wheel.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.question: Which form of paracoccidioidomycosis is more common and primarily affects older men?
items.explanation: The chronic or adult form of paracoccidioidomycosis accounts for ~90% of cases and predominantly affects older men.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.question: Which treatment is considered the first choice for paracoccidioidomycosis due to its longer-standing experience?
items.explanation: Itraconazole is the treatment of choice for paracoccidioidomycosis owing to its longer-standing experience in this disease.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a risk factor for developing talaromycosis?
items.explanation: Talaromycosis occurs most often among persons living in rural areas where bamboo rats are found, not urban areas.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · TALAROMYCOSIS

items.question: What is the primary method of transmission for Talaromyces marneffei to humans?
options: Direct contact with infected bamboo rats
Inhalation of conidia from the environment
items.explanation: Infection is presumed to result from inhalation of conidia from the environment.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · TALAROMYCOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method can quickly establish a presumptive diagnosis for talaromycosis?
options: Culture of T. marneffei on blood agar
items.explanation: Histopathologic examination can quickly establish a presumptive diagnosis by identifying oval or elliptical yeast-like organisms.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · TALAROMYCOSIS

items.question: Which *Emergomyces* species is most geographically widespread?
items.explanation: *E. pasteurianus* is the most geographically widespread species.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · EMERGOMYCOSIS (DISSEMINATED EMMONSIOSIS)

items.question: Which of the following clinical manifestations is rare in disseminated *Emergomyces* infection?
items.explanation: Dissemination to the CNS is rare in disseminated *Emergomyces* infection.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · EMERGOMYCOSIS (DISSEMINATED EMMONSIOSIS)

items.question: Which diagnostic method may lead to misidentification of *Emergomyces* species?
items.explanation: The yeast form on histopathology may be misidentified as *Histoplasma capsulatum*.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · EMERGOMYCOSIS (DISSEMINATED EMMONSIOSIS)

items.question: Which mold is predominantly responsible for causing adiaspiromycosis?
items.explanation: Adiaspiromycosis is predominantly caused by the mold Emmonia crescens.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · ADIASPIROMYCOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.question: What is a major risk factor for adiaspiromycosis?
items.explanation: Occupational dust exposure is a major risk factor for adiaspiromycosis.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · ADIASPIROMYCOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.question: What is the typical immune response in tissues affected by adiaspiromycosis?
items.explanation: Adiaspiromycosis leads to the development of noncaseating granulomas in affected tissues.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · ADIASPIROMYCOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.question: Which of the following fungi is NOT commonly reported to cause human infection?
items.explanation: Candida is not listed among the common pigmented molds causing human infections in phaeohyphomycoses.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.question: What was a significant cause of CNS infections during an outbreak in 2012?
items.explanation: The outbreak in 2012 was caused by the injection of methylprednisolone contaminated with Exserohilum species.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.question: Which gene is associated with susceptibility to disseminated and brain phaeohyphomycosis in immunocompetent patients?
items.explanation: Approximately 70% of immunocompetent patients with disseminated and brain phaeohyphomycosis carried deleterious variants in CLEC7A.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.question: Which antifungal agent is preferred for CNS infections due to its adequate concentration at that site?
items.explanation: Voriconazole is the preferred azole for CNS involvement because it reaches adequate concentrations in that anatomic site.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.question: Which Fusarium species is most commonly responsible for human infections?
items.explanation: The passage states that strains of the $F. solani$ complex are most commonly responsible for human infections.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What is a critical cell type for protection against fusariosis?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that neutrophils are the most critical cells for protection against fusariosis.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which diagnostic clue is extremely helpful in identifying disseminated fusariosis?
options: Growth on agar with characteristic macroconidia
items.explanation: The passage indicates that growth in blood cultures, which are positive in as many as 50% of patients with disseminated fusariosis, is an extremely helpful diagnostic clue.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which antifungal agent is most active against Fusarium species?
items.explanation: The passage states that AmB is the most active antifungal drug against Fusarium.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which investigational antifungal agent inhibits fungal Gwt1 and glycosylphosphatidylinositol-mannoprotein synthesis?
items.explanation: Fosmanogepix inhibits fungal Gwt1 and glycosylphosphatidylinositol-mannoprotein synthesis.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 2) | SCEDOSPORIOSIS AND LOMENTOSPORIOSIS

items.question: Which species is more geographically restricted with a greater representation of reported infections from Australia and Spain?
items.explanation: Lomentospora prolificans is more geographically restricted with a greater representation of reported infections from Australia and Spain.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 2) | SCEDOSPORIOSIS AND LOMENTOSPORIOSIS

items.question: Which infection mimics aspergillosis both clinically and in appearance on CT scan?
items.explanation: Scedosporium apiospermum complex pulmonary infection mimics aspergillosis both clinically and in appearance on CT scan.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 2) | SCEDOSPORIOSIS AND LOMENTOSPORIOSIS

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.statement: Paracoccidioidomycosis is most commonly diagnosed in women.
items.explanation: There is a striking male-to-female ratio ranging from 14:1 to as high as 70:1, indicating that it predominantly affects men.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.statement: The acute form of paracoccidioidomycosis typically develops in individuals over 30 years old.
items.explanation: The acute or juvenile form of paracoccidioidomycosis is uncommon and typically develops in persons under 30 years old.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.statement: Direct transmission of talaromycosis from bamboo rats to humans has been documented.
items.explanation: There is no evidence for direct transmission of the infection from rats to humans.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · TALAROMYCOSIS

items.statement: Amphotericin B (AmB) is used as initial treatment for severe talaromycosis.
items.explanation: Severe infection should be treated with AmB until clinical improvement occurs.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · TALAROMYCOSIS

items.statement: *Emergomyces* has been cultured from the environment.
items.explanation: *Emergomyces* has yet to be cultured from the environment.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · EMERGOMYCOSIS (DISSEMINATED EMMONSIOSIS)

items.statement: Diagnosis of *Emergomyces* infection can be made with a commercially available Histoplasma antigen test.
items.explanation: The Histoplasma antigen test has high cross-reactivity and is not definitive for Emergomyces.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · EMERGOMYCOSIS (DISSEMINATED EMMONSIOSIS)

items.statement: E. crescens has been successfully cultured from human tissue with adiaspiromycosis.
items.explanation: Diagnosis of adiaspiromycosis depends on histopathologic identification as E. crescens has not been cultured from human tissue.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · ADIASPIROMYCOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.statement: Adiaspores can bud and replicate within host tissues.
items.explanation: Adiaspores are incapable of budding or replication once they enlarge in host tissue.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · ADIASPIROMYCOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.statement: Amphotericin B (AmB) is effective against all dematiaceous fungi.
items.explanation: AmB is not effective against many of the organisms causing phaeohyphomycosis.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.statement: Disseminated and focal visceral infections with brown-black molds have low mortality rates.
items.explanation: These types of infections, especially those involving the CNS, are associated with high mortality rates.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.statement: Disseminated fusariosis has a lower mortality rate compared to aspergillosis.
items.explanation: The passage states that the mortality rates for disseminated fusariosis can be as high as 85%, which is comparable or higher than those of disseminated aspergillosis.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Fusarium species are resistant to many antifungal agents.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that Fusarium species are resistant to many antifungal agents.

items.statement: Scedosporium apiospermum infections can occur in immunocompetent patients following near-drowning accidents.
items.explanation: Infections of the lungs and CNS from S. apiospermum have been described in immunocompetent patients after near-drowning.

items.statement: Lomentospora prolificans can be easily differentiated from Aspergillus based on morphology in tissues.

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.assertion: Most patients with paracoccidioidomycosis are middle-aged or elderly men from rural areas.
items.reason: The highest incidence of Paracoccidioides infections is seen in Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, and Ecuador.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why most patients are middle-aged or elderly men from rural areas.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.subtopic: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS
items.assertion: Serious cases of paracoccidioidomycosis should initially be treated with itraconazole.
items.reason: Itraconazole is the treatment of choice for paracoccidioidomycosis due to its longer-standing experience in this disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because seriously ill patients should initially be treated with amphotericin B, not itraconazole.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections | ENDEMIC MYCOSES (DIMORPHIC FUNGI) | PARACOCCIDIOIDOMYCOSIS

items.assertion: Clinical sequelae such as respiratory failure in adiaspiromycosis are caused by the mold itself.
items.reason: The host's granulomatous inflammatory response to infected tissue causes clinical sequelae like respiratory failure.
items.explanation: Respiratory failure is due to the host's inflammatory response, not directly from the mold.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · ADIASPIROMYCOSIS | MOLD INFECTIONS

items.assertion: Disseminated phaeohyphomycosis may occur in patients with solid organ or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
items.reason: These patients are often immunocompromised, increasing susceptibility to opportunistic infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why disseminated phaeohyphomycosis may occur in these patients.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.assertion: Surgical debridement is unnecessary for treating phaeohyphomycosis.
items.reason: Antifungal treatment alone can manage localized and disseminated infections effectively.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; surgical debridement is vital in addition to antifungal treatment.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · PHAEOHYPHOMYCOSES

items.assertion: Patients with chronic granulomatous disease (CGD) have a higher risk of fusariosis compared to aspergillosis.
items.reason: Patients with CGD carry mutations that impair the phagocyte oxidative burst, making them susceptible to infections like fusariosis.
items.explanation: The passage states that patients with CGD are at risk for fusariosis but with far lower frequency compared to aspergillosis.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Disseminated fusariosis can occur secondary to pre-existing local disease.
items.reason: Local diseases such as sinusitis or onychomycosis can lead to hematogenous dissemination of Fusarium.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that disseminated disease can occur secondary to pre-existing local disease, such as sinusitis or onychomycosis.
items.reference: Uncommon Disseminated Fungal Infections · FUSARIOSIS (part 1)
